steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.iste: 'iste' is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'that (of yours)'

hints.to play: 'to play' is 'ludere' — 'eludere' adds the prefix e- meaning 'to mock/evade'
hints.to wash: 'to wash' is 'eluere' — similar prefix but different root verb
hints.to praise: 'to praise' is 'laudare' — a different verb entirely

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'eludit' — 'eludet' has the future -et ending for 3rd conjugation

hints.ludo, ludere, lusi, lusum: These are the parts of 'ludere' (to play) — the base verb without the e- prefix
hints.eluo, eluere, elui, elutum: These are the parts of 'eluere' (to wash out) — a different verb with similar prefix
hints.laudo, laudare, laudavi, laudatum: These are the parts of 'laudare' (to praise) — entirely different verb

hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'furoris' — 'furor' is the nominative subject
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'furorem' — 'furor' is the subject here
hints.Vocative: Cicero is not addressing madness — 'furor' is the subject of 'eludet'

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns typically end in -a — 'furor' with gen. 'furoris' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-er — 'furor' is 3rd declension (gen. furoris)
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns end in -us with gen. -us — 'furor' has gen. 'furoris' (3rd)

hints.Nominative: 'nos' can be nominative ('we'), but here it's the direct object of 'eludet'
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'nobis' — 'nos' as object of 'eludet' is accusative
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'nobis' — 'nos' here is accusative, the direct object

steps.prompt: What does the demonstrative 'iste' specifically connote?

hints.Neutral reference — 'that': 'is/ea/id' is the neutral demonstrative — 'iste' carries a pejorative tone in oratory
hints.Proximity — 'this here': 'hic/haec/hoc' indicates proximity — 'iste' refers to something belonging to the addressee, often with disdain
hints.Distance — 'that over there': 'ille/illa/illud' marks distance — 'iste' specifically associates with the 2nd person, often disparagingly

steps.prompt: This question parallels the opening 'Quo usque tandem.' What rhetorical technique is this repetition of similar questions?

hints.Litotes: Litotes is understatement by negation — these are emphatic repeated questions
hints.Chiasmus: Chiasmus is an ABBA word-order reversal — this is accumulation of parallel questions
hints.Hyperbaton: Hyperbaton is unusual word order — the technique here is piling up similar rhetorical questions

translation: How long will that madness of yours still mock us?
